coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections have emerged as a significant global public health challenge that requires urgent attention. In the research of popular antimicrobial agents, antimicrobial peptide mimics with good properties have the disadvantage of high toxicity, and nanomaterials with metal-doped carbon dots as the most representative have the problems of easy agglomeration and insufficient bactericidal effect. Herein, combined therapeutic strategy was proposed to reach the best compromise and sterilization effects. We employed an electrostatic co-assembly strategy to combine nanomaterials iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) and antimicrobial polypeptoids Poly(N-allylglycine) modified with thiol-terminated amines (PNAG66-NH2), resulting in the creation of the antimicrobial composite Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. Through electrostatic adsorption, the composite disrupts the electrostatic environment of the bacterial outer membrane, alters its permeability, and triggers an increase in intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) to rapidly kill 99.999% of Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) within 10 min. It exhibited negligible cytotoxicity to normal cells. Furthermore, in vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 accelerated the healing of infected wounds, reduced inflammation. The present study demonstrates that the efficient bactericidal properties of the complexes are triggered by the synergistic action of nanomaterials and antimicrobial polypeptoids, which provides a new strategy to achieve safe and efficient broad-spectrum bactericidal activity in antimicrobial aspects.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Fe-CDs, PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. (a) Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 formed via Co-assembly between the positively charged PNAG66-NH2 and the negatively charged Fe-CDs. (b) Representative 1H NMR spectra of PNAG66-NH2 in D2O solvent. (c) FTIR spectra of PNAG66-NH2. (d) TEM images and the particle size distribution (inset) for Fe-CDs. (e) XPS spectra of Fe 2p. (f) FTIR spectra of Fe-CDs. (g) Representative TEM image of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. (h) Zeta potential characterization of components Fe-CDs, PNAG66-NH2, and Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. (i) EIS characterizations were conducted in a solution containing 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- with 100 mM KCl. Error bars indicate the standard deviation of three independent measurements (n = 3).

Antimicrobial activity of different materials and their biocompatibility. (a) Antimicrobial activity of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 with different monomer ratios against E. coli. (b) Antimicrobial activity of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 with different monomer ratios against S. aureus. (c) Cytotoxicity of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 with different monomer ratios on L929 cells. (d) Cytotoxicity of Fe-CDs against L929 cells. (e) Cytotoxicity of PNAG66-NH2 against L929 cells. (f) Representative fluorescence images of live/dead E. coli and S. aureus cells following various treatments (Scale bar = 50 µm). Error bars indicate the standard deviation of three independent measurements (n = 3). Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA analysis. (*p < 0.05, ****p < 0.0001).

Mechanistic study for the antibacterial property of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. (a) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus after incubation with Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 solution. Red arrows indicate pores on the surface of E. coli and S. aureus. (b) Leakage of protein from E. coli and S. aureus after being treated with different material treatments. (c) Cell outer membrane permeability of E. coli and S. aureus with different material treatments. (d) ROS level of E. coli and S. aureus under different material treatments. (e) MDA assay for lipid peroxidation induced by different material treatments in E. coli and S. aureus. (f) Schematic representation of the possible bactericidal mechanisms of Fe-CDs, PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. Error bars indicate the standard deviation of three independent measurements (n = 3). Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA analysis. (****p < 0.0001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The healing process of rat wounds is treated with different material treatments. (a) Schematic diagram of animal experiment process. (b) Photos of wound healing process in the presence of different treatments. (c) Quantitative calculation of the wound area in Fig. 5b. (d) The corresponding changes in body weight of the rats. The data were shown as mean ± S.D. (n = 6 per group). (e) Histological analysis of wound tissue sections stained with H&E on Day 9 post-antibacterial treatment. (f) Histological analysis of wound tissue sections stained with Masson on Day 9 post-antibacterial treatment.

Biosafety of different materials treatments in rats. (a) Blood biochemical indicators including ALT, AST, ALP, TP, ALB, GLB, A/G, BUN, UA, CRE. (b) H&E staining of vital organs of rats after drug treatment. The error bars indicate means ± standard deviation (SD, n = 3). n. s. represented no statistical difference. Student’s T-test.
A schematic overview of the synthesis of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 and its role in inhibiting bacterial growth and promoting wound healing.

head.ce:title: Co-assembly of antimicrobial polypeptoids/carbon dots for internal-external cooperated sterilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections have emerged as a significant global public health challenge that requires urgent attention. In the research of popular antimicrobial agents, antimicrobial peptide mimics with good properties have the disadvantage of high toxicity, and nanomaterials with metal-doped carbon dots as the most representative have the problems of easy agglomeration and insufficient bactericidal effect. Herein, combined therapeutic strategy was proposed to reach the best compromise and sterilization effects. We employed an electrostatic co-assembly strategy to combine nanomaterials iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) and antimicrobial polypeptoids Poly(N-allylglycine) modified with thiol-terminated amines (PNAG66-NH2), resulting in the creation of the antimicrobial composite Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. Through electrostatic adsorption, the composite disrupts the electrostatic environment of the bacterial outer membrane, alters its permeability, and triggers an increase in intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) to rapidly kill 99.999% of Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) within 10 min. It exhibited negligible cytotoxicity to normal cells. Furthermore, in vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 accelerated the healing of infected wounds, reduced inflammation. The present study demonstrates that the efficient bactericidal properties of the complexes are triggered by the synergistic action of nanomaterials and antimicrobial polypeptoids, which provides a new strategy to achieve safe and efficient broad-spectrum bactericidal activity in antimicrobial aspects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria are the main causative agents of many infectious diseases, and bacterial infections are one of the most important contributors to mortality worldwide [1]. Antimicrobial peptide (AMPs) mimetics are desirable antimicrobial therapeutic agents with the advantages of low cost, simple synthesis process and high activity [2]. Peptide mimetics with high antimicrobial activity are usually cationic polymers that interact with negatively charged cell membrane surfaces [3]. However, this electrostatic interaction is also harmful to normal cells. In order to resolve the contradiction between antimicrobial properties and toxicity faced by antimicrobial polypeptoids, combination therapies have received much attention [4,5]. Another popular antimicrobial agent is nanomaterials, which are not prone to triggering bacterial resistance and possess drug delivery capabilities, demonstrating remarkable potential in fighting bacterial infections [6]. Based on the excellent antibacterial ability of polypeptoids and the extensive biological functions of nanomaterials, polypeptoids are loaded onto nanomaterials to construct nanomedicine systems [7,8]. This enables targeted delivery of antimicrobial polypeptoids, increasing their accumulation in bacterial cells, enhancing antimicrobial efficacy and reducing the concentration and potential toxicity of a single ingredient, thus effectively reducing side effects while ensuring efficacy [9–11]. However, the low drug-carrying capacity of some nanocarriers may affect the antibacterial effect of antimicrobial polypeptoids in vivo [12,13]. Therefore, the selection of suitable carriers for the construction of effective co-therapeutic nanomedicine systems is key to achieving low dose, low toxicity, and efficient broad-spectrum bactericidal effects [14,15].

Metal-doped carbon dots (CDs), as one of the most representative types of nanoparticles, can facilitate the entry of functionalized metal substances into cells and initiate important stress responses [16–19]. However, there are certain shortcomings in practical application, metal-doped CDs usually require the use of high concentrations (≥300 μg mL−1), which may lead to aggregation of the CDs and influence their bactericidal effect [20]. Nevertheless, the excellent biocompatibility, high specific surface area and good surface capacity still make metal-doped CDs an ideal nanocarrier for active polypeptoids delivery, which significantly enhances the interaction of polypeptoids with bacteria and facilitates their penetration into cells [21–23]. Meanwhile the disruption of cell membranes by peptide analogues opens up more channels for carbon sites to enter bacteria. Therefore, the combined use of these two not only reduces the toxicity of antimicrobial polypeptoids to mammalian cells, but also allows metal-doped CDs to achieve a stronger antimicrobial effect at low concentrations [24–27]. This strategy aims to circumvent the shortcomings of a single antimicrobial agent, to reach the best compromise and sterilization effects and to open new perspectives for antimicrobial therapy.

In this paper, a novel antibacterial composite (Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2) was successfully constructed by electrostatically co-assembling iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with the cationic polymer poly(N-allylglycine) modified with thiol-terminated amines (PNAG66-NH2), which exhibits antimicrobial peptide (AMPs) properties. The complex was able to effectively shorten the antimicrobial time, improve the antimicrobial efficiency, reduce the amount of antimicrobial agent and enhance the biosafety by taking advantage of the synergistic effect of Fe-CDs and PNAG66-NH2. The Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 complex (0.0625 μg mL−1 PNAG66-NH2 + 50 μg mL−1 Fe-CDs) was able to efficiently kill 99.999 % of Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) within 10 min with negligible cytotoxicity to normal cells. PNAG66-NH2 on the surface of the complex disrupts the electrostatic environment of the bacterial outer membrane by electrostatic adsorption and forms pores, which increases the uptake of Fe-CDs by the bacteria, and Fe-CDs as a carrier can in turn help the entry of PNAG66-NH2 into the bacteria. Once the complex enters the bacteria, it triggers an oxidative stress response that further increases the membrane pores. Because of the complementary internal and external antibacterial mechanisms of Fe-CDs and PNAG66-NH2, a stronger antibacterial effect was achieved. In vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 significantly accelerated the healing of wounds infected by S. aureus in Sprague Dawley (SD) rats (Scheme 1 ). This study demonstrates a co-diagnostic strategy of nanomaterials and antimicrobial polypeptoids with great potential for treating bacterial infections and promoting tissue regeneration, providing new ideas for advancing the development of antimicrobial solutions.
All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the 3R principle and received approval from the Animal Ethics Committee of Qingdao University under approval number QDU-AEC-2024736.

Citric acid monohydrate (CAM, AR), Urea (CO(NH2)2, AR), Ferric Chloride Hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, AR), and Sodium Chloride (NaCl, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Tryptone and Yeast extract were from OXOID. Mueller-Hinton Broth (MH) was from Qingdao HOPEBIO Co. and was used as received. Agar Powder (Agar) was from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. 2.5 % glutaraldehyde fixative (for electron microscopy) was from Maclean's Biochemical Technology Co. Trypsin (TPS, 0.25 %) was from Wuhan Pricella Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum (special grade), phosphate buffered saline (PBS, injection grade) were from Accela ChemBio Co., Ltd. tetrahydrofuran (THF, AR) was from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. 2,2-Dimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (DMPA, >98 %) was obtained from TCI (Shanghai) Chemical Industry Development Co. 2-Aminoethanethiol hydrochloride (C2H7NS·HCl, 98 %), Benzylamine (BZM, 99 %), and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, >99.8 %) were ordered from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. E. coli (ATCC25922) and S. aureus (ATCC25923) were supplied by Qingdao HOPEBIO Co., Ltd. and reactivated according to the instructions provided. SD rats (all-female rats, weighing 250–300 g, aged about 8 weeks) were purchased from Jinan Peng Yue Laboratory Animal Breeding Co. All other chemicals used in the study were of analytical grade and all aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water.

Synthesized by an existing laboratory method for the synthesis of iron-carbon nanodots. The specific preparation method is to dissolve 1.0 g urea, 2.0 g citric acid monohydrate and 1.0 g FeCl3·6H2O in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and microwave for 1 min 45 s. After the microwave heating procedure, the solid condensed at the bottom of the beaker was redispersed with a small amount of deionized water. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to obtain a yellowish-brown clarified liquid. This liquid was transferred to a 500 Da dialysis bag placed in a beaker of deionized water and subjected to dialysis treatment using a magnetic stirrer for 24 h, during which time the deionized water was changed at least four times. After dialysis was completed, the dialysate was poured into a petri dish and dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain a dark brown powder. The obtained material needs to be kept dry and protected from light for spare use.

In a typical procedure [28], the N-allyl N-carboxyanhydride (NCA) (300 mg, 2.12 mmol) dissolved in 3 mL of anhydrous THF, followed by adding a stock solution of benzylamine (3.61 wt% in THF). The solution was stirred at 55 °C for 48 h under nitrogen protection and the progress of polymerization was monitored by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR). The mixture was precipitated into an excess of cold diethyl ether and dried under reduced pressure, yielding a white solid (74.2 % yield).

In a typical procedure based on literature methods [28], 150 mg of PNAG66, cysteamine hydrochloride, and DMPA were dissolved in 5 mL of DMF. The molar ratio of [-SH]: [CC]: [DMPA] was set to 100:20:1. After degassing, the mixture was irradiated with UV light at room temperature for 3 h. The resulting white solid was obtained after dialysis for 3 days and lyophilized (69.5 % yield).
Composites were prepared by electrostatic adsorption of PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs, obtained by stirring in deionized water for 2h.

We performed morphological observations, live/dead bacterial staining, bacterial outer membrane permeability, and protein leakage assays to examine the membrane integrity of both treated and untreated bacteria. External and internal changes of bacteria before and after treatment were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Tescan Vega3, Czechia) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-1400Flash, Japan). Live and dead bacteria were stained using N, N-dimethylaniline N-oxide (DMAO) / Propidium Iodide (PI) Bacterial Viability Assay Kit (Beyotime, China). The extent of damage to the bacterial outer membrane was assessed by measuring its permeability with the hydrophobic fluorescent probe N-Phenyl-1-naphthylamine (NPN). The concentration of leakage proteins from bacterial cells was detected using the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) Protein Content Assay Kit (Boxbio, China). Measurement of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and malondialdehyde (MDA) activity in bacteria to assess oxidative stress using the Reactive Oxygen Specific Assay Kit (Biosharp, China) and the Lipid Oxidation Specific Assay Kit (Beyotime, China). Detailed experimental procedures can be found in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Fe-CDs, PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: The scheme proposed in this experiment is to mix PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs in a 1:1 vol ratio and co-assemble them into the nanocomplex Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 by electrostatic interactions (Fig. 1 a). First, PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs need to be prepared sequentially. PNAG66-NH2 was synthesized by ring-opening polymerization of the monomer NCA and subsequent post-polymerization as previously reported. The subscript represents the average degree of polymerization of PNAG [29]. The chemical structure of PNAG66-NH2 was confirmed by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and FTIR (Fig. 1b-c). According to our previous studies, CDs with small size can be quickly prepared by microwave method [30]. To load more antimicrobial polypeptoids and increase the surface area of the CDs, we slightly modified the previous synthesis method to change its size to 3.27 ± 0.78 nm (Fig. 1d). The morphology of Fe-CDs was further observed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (Fig. S1), indicating the successful synthesis of Fe-CDs. Because of the characteristic vibrations of graphitic carbon, the dominant X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak was observed to be 2θ = 25.74° (Fig. S2). The Raman spectrum (Fig. S3) indicates the carbon hybridization state, with peaks near 1056 and 2522 cm−1 corresponding to disordered and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. In addition, the characteristic peaks at 243–284 nm in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S4) represent π → π* leaps, which correspond to the presence of chromophores such as CC, and the peaks at 326–357 nm represent n → π* leaps, which correspond to the presence of chromophores, such as CN, CO, or CO bonds [31,32]. Similar to other CDs, these Fe-CDs with carbon nuclei show excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties (Fig. S5) [33,34]. The elemental composition and chemical state of Fe-CDs were determined by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S6a-d). As shown in Fig. 1e, the peak at 710.2 eV belongs to the 2p 3/2 orbital electrons of Fe2+ and the oxidation state of Fe2+, and the peaks at 712.0 eV and 723.7 eV belong to the oxidation state of Fe3+ [35], the peak at 715.6 eV is attributed to the overlap of oscillating satellites of iron oxide, demonstrating the successful doping of iron. Fig. 1f represents the FTIR spectra with peaks around 3178, 1558 and 1353 cm−1 assigned to NH, CO/CN and CN stretching vibrations, respectively [36,37], these hydrophilic groups provide Fe-CDs with excellent stability in aqueous environments. The peaks at about 1075 cm−1 are attributed to Fe-O or Fe-N groups, demonstrating the successful formation of Fe coordination compounds.

In order to validate the co-assembly process, the size and surface charge of the nanocomplexes were characterized. TEM showed that the size of the materials became 4.47 ± 1.66 nm, the overall size became larger (Fig. 1g), and the carbon dots were surrounded by a layer of transparent substance (Fig. S7). The zeta potential results show that the potential of the co-assembled complex Fe-CDs-PANG66-NH2 rises dramatically compared to Fe-CDs, with a potential value of −12.17 mV, which we speculate is likely influenced by the potential of PANG66-NH2 [38] (Fig. 1h). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can display the rich interface structure information from the electrochemical point of view by measuring the corresponding current (or potential) response generated by the system [39]. As shown in Fig. 1i, the electron transfer capabilities of the modified electrodes using Nafion/Fe-CDs and Nafion/Fe-CDs-PANG66-NH2 were assessed. The results showed that Fe-CDs modified electrodes exhibited a bigger semicircle diameter, which indicated a higher electron transfer resistance for redox pairs, Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 modified electrodes exhibited a smaller semicircular diameter than Fe-CDs modified electrodes. These results further demonstrate that PNAG66-NH2 co-assembles with Fe-CDs via electrostatic interactions to form complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antimicrobial performance of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 2 a and b, we evaluated the antimicrobial effects of PNAG66-NH2, Fe-CDs and the co-assembled polymer Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 by comparing their killing kinetics against E. coli and S. aureus. Firstly, we fixed the concentration of Fe-CDs at 50 μg mL−1 and controlled the concentration range of PNAG66-NH2 between 0.03125 and 0.25 μg mL−1, and carried out a rapid antimicrobial effect comparison by performing bacterial plate counts at 10 min (Fig. S8). PNAG66-NH2 at 0.03125 μg mL−1 reduced the viability of E. coli and S. aureus by approximately 2 lg colony-forming units (CFU) mL−1, showing weak antimicrobial activity, and increasing the concentration within a certain range did not significantly improve the bactericidal effect. Fe-CDs at 50 μg mL−1 reduced the viability of E. coli by 1.5 lg CFU mL−1, but did not show antimicrobial activity against S. aureus, which is similar to previous findings attributed to the different structure of bacterial cells [40]. PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs (50μg mL−1) were then co-assembled in a 1:1 volumetric electrostatic co-assembly and then used, and surprisingly, it was found that the complexes showed very strong antibacterial activity, decreasing both E. coli and S. aureus by about 3–5 lg CFU mL−1, both of which were highly significant differences when compared to the bactericidal effect of the monomers, this is similar to the findings of Xiong et al. [41]. Interestingly, the concentration of PNAG66-NH2 is not as high as it should be, and there is an optimal ratio of Fe-CDs (50 μg mL−1)-PNAG66-NH2 (0.0625 μg mL−1) polymers that showed the best antimicrobial efficacy, reducing the viability of both E. coli and S. aureus by about 5 lg CFU mL−1, when PNAG66-NH2 was above or below 0.0625 μg mL−1, the performance of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 on bacteria was attenuated. The in vitro cytotoxicity results of normal cells going with this concentration showed (Fig. 2c) that the Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 complex exhibited negligible cytotoxicity to L929 cells (mouse fibroblasts), with a survival rate of 100.0 %, indicating very good biosafety.

The antimicrobial performance of Fe-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus was assessed by 30 min standard CFU (Fig. S9). Fe-CDs reduced E. coli viability by about 3 lg CFU mL−1 at 50 μg mL−1 and by about 5 lg CFU mL-1at 400 μg mL−1, neither of which had an observed effect on S. aureus viability. Fig. 2d shows 100.0 % cell survival at 50 μg mL−1 Fe-CDs and only 70.0 % cell survival at 400 μg mL−1, although the bactericidal effect was evident. Fe-CDs take longer and are more cytotoxic to cells in order to achieve the same E. coli killing effect as Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. The antimicrobial activity of PNAG66-NH2 was next evaluated by using serial dilution techniques including minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) determination, and MIC of 16 μg mL−1 and 10 μg mL−1 were measured for E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. S10). Zhang et al. found similar results [42]. It was also found that the MIC was equal to the Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC) (Fig. S11), indicating that PNAG66-NH2 possessed significant bactericidal activity against both E. coli and S. aureus at lower concentrations. It is noteworthy that PNAG66-NH2 is more effective against Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria, despite their thinner cell walls. In vitro cytotoxicity results using this concentration however showed only 61.5 % and 60.8 % viability of normal cells, i.e. very toxic to normal mammalian cells (Fig. 2e). This suggests that the two antimicrobial agents have the disadvantages of low safety, insufficient broad spectrum and poor bactericidal efficacy when used individually, whereas the synergistic use of the two agents can well avoid these problems and has a great potential to address bacterial infections.

To understand the antibacterial mechanism of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2, we continue to discuss the mode of action using E. coli and S. aureus as examples. It should be noted that the next experiments were performed under Fe-CDs (50 μg mL−1)-PNAG66-NH2 (0.0625μg mL−1). The main cause of bacterial death or damage is usually the leakage of cytoplasmic contents due to bacterial cell membrane damage. We use DMAO/PI to stain live/dead bacteria, DMAO has membrane permeability and can penetrate all bacterial cell membranes, PI can only penetrate necrotic bacteria with damaged cell membranes, when DMAO and PI are used together to detect the live/dead bacteria, live bacteria with intact cell membranes exhibit green fluorescence, while dead bacteria with compromised membranes display both green and red fluorescence simultaneously [43]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the blank control group had intense green fluorescence and a small amount of red fluorescence. However, only faint green fluorescence was observed in E. coli and S. aureus treated with Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2, and a large amount of red fluorescence was present. This verifies that Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 has excellent antimicrobial efficiency and may disrupt cell membranes, which needs to be further investigated.

The interaction between Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 and bacterial membranes was further investigated by SEM and TEM. In SEM, untreated E. coli had a complete shape, smooth surface and clear and intact membrane, whereas S. aureus was spherical with an intact shape. Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2-treated E. coli showed constriction, depression at both ends and severe deformation of the overall morphology, whereas S. aureus showed similar features with surface depressions. However, because the thick cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria provides a sturdy protection to the bacteria, in some cases, despite the fact that the membrane has been severely broken, the thickness and strength of the cell wall makes the overall breakage may not be as obvious (Fig. 3 a). TEM images showed that bacterial cells treated with Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 had a lower lining, hollow interior and loss of cellular integrity compared to untreated bacterial cells, suggesting that part of the cytoplasm underwent significant exocytosis (Fig. S12). SEM and TEM images were consistent in that the bacterial damages could be seen directly, initially verifying the conjecture of structural integrity of bacterial cells. It suggests that the contact of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 with the bacteria resulted in structural disruption of the bacterial membrane, increased permeability, and leakage of internal material, leading to bacterial death. A clear sign of bacterial membrane damage is the leakage of intracellular substances, especially the loss of some important proteins, and we used the BCA method to detect the concentration of proteins leaked from the bacteria after material treatment (Fig. 3b). Protein leakage from Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2-treated E. coli was significantly increased, exceeding that under the treatment of the monomer. As Fe-CDs were less effective against S. aureus, protein leakage was similar to that of the control, but under Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 treatment, protein leakage of S. aureus was significantly increased, suggesting that the cell membrane was severely disrupted. This is consistent with previous findings that there is a synergistic effect between Fe-CDs and PNAG66-NH2 and that the combination of the two leads to greater loss of bacterial content and accelerated bacterial death. It is worth noting that protein leakage was slightly higher in S. aureus than in E. coli, which may be due to the fact that S. aureus can produce a variety of exogenous toxins (e.g., α-hemolysin, enterotoxin, etc.). The release of these toxins may be significantly increased when bacterial membranes are damaged, and S. aureus is capable of initiating the expression of specific genes and increasing the number of proteins secreted within the cell in the stressful state [44,45].

In addition, the membrane permeability of bacteria was further assessed by NPN, which is specifically designed to detect membrane permeability, and it does not enter intact cells and only enters injured cells [46]. As shown in Fig. 3c, Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 showed the highest fluorescence intensity produced by NPN, revealing more disruption of bacterial membranes by Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 treatment. The addition of PNAG66-NH2 increased the surface electrostatic effect of Fe-CDs and enhanced the adsorption to bacteria, resulting in a more severe disintegration of the cell membrane. However, such severe bacterial cell damage resulting from PNAG66-NH2 contact alone is difficult to interpret. ROS are known to be one of the ways in which nanomaterials lead to bacterial death, and ROS are able to remove hydrogen atoms from the long-chain double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids, leading to lipid peroxidation. Most metal nanoparticles achieve bactericidal effects by oxidatively damaging essential cellular components [47,48]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the levels of ROS were significantly increased inside Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 treated E. coli and S. aureus compared to untreated and treated with monomer group. The membrane-disrupting effect of PNAG66-NH2 increased bacterial uptake of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2, helped Fe-CDs to translocate more easily across the bacterial membrane, triggered a surge in ROS, and significantly improved bactericidal activity. Rapid accumulation of ROS inside the bacterial cell for a short period of time can lead to lipid oxidation in the cell and generate MDA. Bacteria treated with Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 showed a significantly higher concentration of MDA than bacteria treated with both monomers, especially in S. aureus (Fig. 3e). It is speculated that it may be due to the fact that Gram-positive bacteria have a unique structural feature, the special lipoteichoic acid (LTA), which antimicrobial peptides have been reported to tend to bind [49]. Thus, LTA may act as a ladder to help complex cross the cytoplasmic membrane from the outside.

This interesting result explains the antimicrobial mechanism of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 combined with previous results (Fig. 3f). Electrostatically co-assembled Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 form a more compact structure than the two monomers, with the positively charged PNAG66-NH2 on the surface adhering to the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane surface. By forming pores on the surface through membrane perturbation, Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 can more easily enter into the inside of bacterial cells, and increase the intracellular ROS level, and lipid peroxidation occurs, which further ruptures the bacterial membrane, and irreversible cell death occurs as a result of the outflow of the inclusions. PNAG66-NH2 enhanced the permeability of Fe-CDs to the bacteria, while Fe-CDs enhanced the destructive effect of PNAG66-NH2 on the bacterial membrane and loaded PNAG66-NH2 into bacteria, both synergistically sterilized the bacteria, dismantling them both internally and externally. The results of the study significantly showed that the combination therapy of PNAG66-NH2 loaded on Fe-CDs exhibited excellent antibacterial activity, effectively inhibiting the growth and survival of both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo bacterial infected wound closure
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo therapeutic potential of the combination therapy in S. aureus infected wounds was evaluated in a SD rat model encouraged by the excellent antibacterial activity and good biocompatibility of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 (Fig. 4 a). A circular wound measuring 1 cm in diameter was formed on the back of each rat (Fig. S13), which was randomly assigned to four groups following 24 h of S. aureus infection and subjected to different treatments: (1) Sterile water, (2) PNAG66-NH2, (3) Fe-CDs, and (4) Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2. Day 1, 4, 7, and 9 photographs of the rat's wound photographs are shown in Fig. 4b, respectively.

At the beginning of treatment (Day 1), the size of each group of wounds remained 1 cm in diameter. It can be seen that on Day 4, the wound areas of all four groups were significantly reduced. On Day 7, the relative wound area was reduced to 32.0 %, and the treated wounds were slightly smaller than those of the other groups; on Day 9, the relative wound area of the rat was reduced to 13.4 %, and the wounds were significantly healed, much less than those of the other treated groups (Fig. 4c). In addition, the rat did not develop ulcers or pus during the treatment. Additionally, the body weight of rats in each group showed an increasing trend after treatment, which indicated that these materials had no toxic side effects in vivo [50] (Fig. 4d). As shown in Fig. 4e, Wound tissues from rat skin wounds under different groups of treatments were subjected to H&E staining microscopy on Day 9. Obvious skin loss was visible in the control, PNAG66-NH2 and Fe-CDs groups, with incomplete skin tissue structure, fragmented epidermis, no typical dermis layer, necrosis occurred at the edge of the tissue, and many inflammatory cells were distributed in the wounds, showing obvious unrepaired areas, suggesting that the skin damage was still continuing[51,52]. In the Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 group, the skin tissue structure at the wound was intact, and the epidermis was covered with keratin, indicating that the epidermal structure of the skin was beginning to form, and a large number of fibroblasts were observed, with only a small number of inflammatory cells distributed, and there were no obvious hemorrhages indicating that the organism was repairing the broken skin tissues [53], and the healing effect was significantly better than that in the control group, the PNAG66-NH2 group, and the Fe-CDs group. The results showed that the combination therapy of Fe-CDs with PNAG66-NH2 had very excellent antimicrobial and wound-healing-promoting abilities in vivo. Masson staining revealed blue coloration indicative of collagen fiber deposition, a key indicator of re-epithelialization during skin wound healing [54]. The Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 group exhibited a notable increase in collagen fiber content compared to the other groups (Fig. 4f), with little difference in collagen deposition among the other three groups, suggesting that the Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 group had the best healing of skin tissue repair. Overall, the results of hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E) and Masson staining showed that the combined treatment of Fe-CDs and PNAG66-NH2 effectively inhibit bacterial growth and promote wound healing by reducing inflammation, promoting cell proliferation, and facilitating re-epithelialization, suggesting that the combination therapy has important applications in combating bacterial infection and promoting healing.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the following 10 biochemical indices were examined in rats: blood alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), total protein (TP), urea-albumin (ALB), globulin (GLB), albumin and globulin (A/G), urea nitrogen (BUN), uric acid (UA) and creatinine (CRE). The blood routine was assessed in different groups without significant significance. In addition, the different groups of treated organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were assessed using H&E staining (Fig. 5b), none of which showed significant damage or abnormalities. These results further demonstrate that this combination therapy, which loads the antimicrobial polypeptoids PNAG66-NH2 onto the nanocarriers Fe-CDs, has excellent biocompatibility and fewer adverse reactions in the organism after treating wounds, highlighting its potential as a promising candidate for wound disinfection.

In summary, we assembled nanomaterials Fe-CDs and antibacterial polypeptoids PNAG66-NH2 by electrostatic interactions, and exploited the synergistic effect of the two to achieve rapid and effective bacterial inhibition. This enables PNAG66-NH2 to inactivate 99.999 % of bacteria within 10 min at a concentration of 0.0625μg mL−1. Significantly improved biosafety compared to other antimicrobial peptide analogues[55,56]. PNAG66-NH2 on the surface of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 was able to disrupt the electrostatic environment of the bacterial outer membrane through electrostatic interactions with the acidic phosphate head groups of bacterial membrane phospholipids, enhance the adhesion ability with the bacterial membrane, and create pores in the membrane through mechanisms such as membrane perturbation, thereby enhancing the permeability of the bacterial membrane. The presence of Fe-CDs enabled PNAG66-NH2, which could only stay on the surface of the cell membrane, to enter the bacterial interior smoothly. The entry of Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 into the bacterial interior initiates an internal ROS surge and leads to lipid peroxidation. This further compromises the integrity of the bacterial membrane and leads to increased leakage of cellular contents. In addition, Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 also showed excellent biosecurity, and in vivo experiments demonstrated that it was effective in promoting the recovery of wounds in S. aureus-infected rats. The complex we constructed solves the challenge of balancing the high efficiency and biosafety of traditional antimicrobial agents and provides a new idea for the design of safe and efficient antimicrobial agents for clinical applications[57–59]. Due to its low cost and easy preparation, Fe-CDs-PNAG66-NH2 has a broad prospect for scale-up applications. This work not only holds great potential in treating bacterial infections and promoting tissue regeneration, but also opens up new directions for solving problems associated with bacterial infections and advancing the use of co-diagnostics.
